nativeRange.habitat: Found in inshore reef-associated environments, including coral reefs and tide pools, at depths ranging from 0 to 85 meters.

diet.notes: Generally fed meaty foods such as fresh or frozen shrimp, squid, scallop, and marine fish flesh. Target feeding two to three times per week has been reported as effective. In the wild, the species has been documented feeding on bottom-dwelling invertebrates and small fish.

goodWith: other large fish too big to swallow
avoidWith: small fish that fit in its mouth

summary: The Marbled Bamboo Cat Shark is a large, nocturnal bottom-dweller from Indo-Pacific reefs, widely considered expert-level due to its size — reaching around 100 to 113 cm in captivity — specialized substrate needs, and sensitivity to copper-based medications.
careNotes:
The Marbled Bamboo Cat Shark (Chiloscyllium punctatum) is a nocturnal, bottom-dwelling shark that tends to hide during daylight hours and becomes active at night. It generally requires a soft sand substrate, as coarser materials have been known to cause abrasion injuries to the ventral (underside) surface, which can lead to infection. Caves or darkened areas are advisable so it can shelter during the day.

In the wild, this species can reach a maximum length of around 132 cm, though hobby sources commonly cite around 102–104 cm as a practical adult size in captive settings. Either way, the tank size requirement is substantial — a minimum of around 896 liters is widely advised, and larger is better.

Like all elasmobranchs (the group that includes sharks and rays), this species is highly sensitive to copper-based medications. Copper treatments must be avoided in any system housing this shark.

This species is oviparous, meaning females deposit egg cases rather than bearing live young. Captive breeding has been documented rarely, and eggs have occasionally appeared in the trade. It has been reported to survive up to 12 hours out of water, though this should not be treated as routine handling guidance.

This species is sold under a wide variety of trade names — Bamboo Shark, Brownbanded Bamboo Shark, Brown-banded Catshark, Marble Cat Shark, and Blackbanded Cat Shark are all names used in the trade for Chiloscyllium punctatum.

Juvenile specimens of this species display distinct black-and-white banding and are sometimes sold separately under names such as "Black Banded Cat Shark" or "Banded Bamboo Shark". The bands fade gradually into the marbled adult coloration over the first one to two years. The care requirements are identical at all life stages.
breedingNotes: Oviparous — females deposit egg cases in the environment. Eggs have occasionally appeared in the aquarium trade (sold separately). Captive breeding has been reported in large public aquarium exhibits and rarely in home aquaria. Detailed captive breeding protocols are not well-documented in hobbyist literature.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Collections page confirmed care level (Expert Only), temperament (Aggressive), and trade listing. Species-specific detail page not directly accessible; supplemented with additional hobbyist sources.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed scientific name, taxonomy (family Hemiscylliidae, order Orectolobiformes), native range (Indo-West Pacific, India to Japan/Australia), wild max size 132 cm, preferred temperature 24.6–29.1°C, reef-associated habitat 0–85 m depth, oviparous reproduction.

additional.name: AquariumDomain
additional.notes: Provided adult size (40 in / 101.6 cm), temp 72–80°F (22.2–26.7°C), pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, care level Expert, temperament semi-aggressive, carnivore diet. Conflicting tank size: quick facts state 350 gal (1,325 L); article text states 180 gal (681 L).

additional.name: FreshMarine
additional.notes: Provided adult size (40.9 in / 103.9 cm), temp 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C), pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, min tank 180 gal (681 L), care level Expert Only, temperament Aggressive, carnivore diet. Used to cross-verify high-stakes parameters.

fish.breedingNotes: Oviparous; egg cases have been sold in the trade and hatched in aquaria. True captive breeding in home tanks is rarely if ever reported; breeding is considered not practical for home aquarists.
